I can tell you, if you want, the rules that apply to Elon Musk=E2=80=99s pa=
y as chief executive officer of Tesla Inc. under Delaware corporate law. Th=
ey are long and boring, a lot of exceptions to exceptions, but to quickly s=
ummarize:=C2=A0The board can pay Musk whatever it wants,=C2=A0unless=C2=A0h=
e is a =E2=80=9Ccontrolling shareholder,=E2=80=9D in which case the pay has=
 to be =E2=80=9Centirely fair,=E2=80=9D though if the shareholders vote to =
approve the pay then that probably makes it =E2=80=9Centirely fair,=E2=80=
=9D unless the shareholder vote is not fully informed, in which case a judg=
e gets to decide if it was =E2=80=9Centirely fair,=E2=80=9D which requires =
a =E2=80=9Cfair process=E2=80=9D in which the board negotiates with Musk at=
 arm=E2=80=99s length, and also a =E2=80=9Cfair price=E2=80=9D in which, yo=
u know, the judge decides if Musk is worth the money.=C2=A0
This week a judge=C2=A0decided that Musk is=C2=A0not=C2=A0worth the $55.8 b=
illion that Tesla=E2=80=99s board agreed to pay him in 2018, and wrote a 20=
0-page opinion explaining and applying those rules. You can read the opinio=
n, or my post about it yesterday, for a fuller explanation of these rules a=
nd how the judge applied them. But I would say that my two main points yest=
erday were:
=09There is some advantage to having a set of outside rules of good behavio=
r and fair treatment of shareholders, upheld by impartial judges, rather th=
an just letting corporate executives and boards and controlling shareholder=
s do whatever they want, but=09Nonetheless, it is=C2=A0weird=C2=A0that Tesl=
a=E2=80=99s management and board of directors and (a large majority of)=C2=
=A0shareholders all agreed that Musk should get paid $55.8 billion for crea=
ting $600 billion of shareholder value, and he did do that, and he got paid=
 that, and a judge overruled that decision and ordered him to give back the=
 money. I can see why Musk =E2=80=94=C2=A0and Tesla=E2=80=99s board, and it=
s shareholders =E2=80=94=C2=A0would find that objectionable! They=E2=80=99r=
e trying to run a company here.

The judge who ruled against Musk=C2=A0is the head of the  Delaware Court of=
 Chancery, the specialized court in Delaware that hears corporate law dispu=
tes, and she heard the dispute about Musk=E2=80=99s pay because Tesla, like=
 many US public companies, is incorporated in Delaware. (Even though its fa=
ctories and offices are mostly located elsewhere.) Lots of public companies=
 are incorporated in Delaware, for a combination of reasons:
=09Delaware has a specialized court that hears corporate law disputes, the =
Court of Chancery.=C2=A0The judges on that court (the chancellor and vice-c=
hancellors) are=C2=A0experts, they hear a lot of corporate law disputes, th=
ey understand the issues, and they mostly make sensible decisions. They als=
o understand that these cases are time-sensitive, so they move fast. (Thoug=
h in Musk=E2=80=99s case the decision did take rather a long time.) They al=
so don=E2=80=99t have juries.=C2=A0So if there=E2=80=99s some dispute about=
 what a Delaware public company can do, the company knows it can go to cour=
t and get a quick answer from a smart, knowledgeable judge.=09That court ha=
s been around=C2=A0for a long time, so there are a lot of precedents, so De=
laware law is=C2=A0predictable. I=C2=A0can=C2=A0tell you the rules that app=
ly to Elon Musk=E2=80=99s pay, and for each of the debatable terms =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9Ccontrolling shareholder,=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9Centirely fair,=
=E2=80=9D etc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0there are cases that explain how to interpret=
 them. Predictability=C2=A0is very important to public companies. They don=
=E2=80=99t=C2=A0want=C2=A0to go to court to get answers about what they can=
 do: They want to=C2=A0know=C2=A0what they can do, in advance, without gett=
ing sued. If you=E2=80=99re a Delaware company and you have some gnarly iss=
ue, you can call pretty much any big-time corporate lawyer and say =E2=80=
=9Cam I allowed to do this gnarly thing,=E2=80=9D and she will go consult D=
elaware precedents and come back to you with a pretty good answer.
  [1]
=C2=A0Note that in Delaware, as elsewhere, this is largely a matter of judg=
e-made law, of precedential rulings interpreting=C2=A0the rules; it=E2=80=
=99s not like the Delaware legislature (or any other state=E2=80=99s legisl=
ature) sat down and wrote detailed rules about how much=C2=A0a company can =
pay its CEO. But compared to other states, Delaware=E2=80=99s judge-made co=
rporate law is more detailed and predictable.=09In some very general way, D=
elaware law is pretty business-friendly, and specifically pretty friendly t=
o corporate management. If you ask your fancy lawyer =E2=80=9Cam I allowed =
to do this gnarly thing,=E2=80=9D there=E2=80=99s a decent chance that the =

=E2=80=99s possible that some judge or jury will find this thing distastefu=

=99s case though.

That third point is, I want to say, probably the least important factor on =
the list. If you are deciding where to incorporate your company, knowing th=

ant=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0is probably less important than knowing that=C2=
=A0in general=C2=A0the rules are predictable and reasonable, that you can r=

=A0
On the other hand, if you are another US state (or a foreign jurisdiction),=

the Class Period, Defendants made similar statements, consistently assuring=

and transparency.=E2=80=9D Defendants also claimed that Boeing did not make=
 =E2=80=9Ctrade-offs,=E2=80=9D between safety and profit, and that safety h=
as =E2=80=9Calways been the priority and that will continue to be=E2=80=9D =
at Boeing.
Unbeknownst to investors, statements such as those above were false and mis=
leading because Boeing failed to disclose that it had been prioritizing its=
 profits over safety, which led to poor quality control standards in the pr=
oduction of its commercial aircrafts such as the 737 MAX, resulting in a he=
ightened risk of manufacturing flaws which could render the Company=E2=80=
=99s new airplanes unsafe. This very risk had materialized during the Class=
 Period.

That door flew off on a Friday, and the next Monday the stock dropped by 8%=
, destroying about $12 billion of market capitalization. There were 177 pas=
sengers on the flight, and they each got $1,500 of compensation for the tra=
uma and inconvenience of being on a plane whose door flew off in midair. I =
suppose some might sue for more. If they all sue and get $50 million each o=
f damages, which seems unlikely, that adds up to like $8.9 billion, still l=
ess than Boeing=E2=80=99s shareholders lost on the news. Boeing=E2=80=99s s=
hareholders are the real victims here, or that is the conclusion of =E2=80=
=9Ceverything is securities fraud=E2=80=9D anyway.

  [1] One thing that I often wondered, as a young mergers-and-acquisitions =
lawyer in New York, licensed to practice New York law, is how we all got aw=
ay with giving companies advice about Delaware merger law. But that=E2=80=
=99s not important right now.


  [2] You could imagine a state trying to distinguish itself by being more =
*shareholder-friendly* than Delaware, but that seems pointless, because gen=
erally the managers choose where to incorporate, not the shareholders. You =
could imagine an argument of the form =E2=80=9CState X=E2=80=99s corporate =
law is more protective of shareholders than Delaware=E2=80=99s is, so it in=
creases the value of State X corporations, so companies should incorporate =
in State X to increase their value by giving up some of the freedom that De=
laware offers,=E2=80=9D but I think in practice that is not very appealing.=
 It is also unlikely mostly due to the points about sensibility and predict=
ability of Delaware law. Shareholders, like managers, will often prefer pre=
dictable sensible rules over uncertain rules that might be more favorable t=
o them.


  [3] I also asked ChatGPT, which gave me a clear, confident but wrong answ=
er: =E2=80=9CIn summary, Texas courts generally apply the business judgment=
 rule, giving substantial deference to the decisions of corporate directors=
 and officers unless evidence of wrongdoing or breach of fiduciary duty is =
present. In contrast, Delaware courts typically use the entire fairness sta=
ndard with enhanced scrutiny, placing a greater burden on corporations and =
their directors to prove the fairness of CEO compensation packages.=E2=80=
=9D But that is not at all true about Delaware law? I mean the chancellor d=
id apply entire fairness in Musk=E2=80=99s case, because he is a controllin=
g shareholder, but that is not generally true, and the first page of her op=
inion says =E2=80=9CA board of director=E2=80=99s decision on how much to p=
ay a company=E2=80=99s chief executive officer is the quintessential busine=
ss determination subject to great judicial deference.=E2=80=9D So I will no=
t trust ChatGPT on this one.


  [4] Incidentally, in that 1984 federal case, the court complained in a fo=
otnote: =E2=80=9CWe are both surprised and inconvenienced by the circumstan=
ce that, despite their multitudinous and voluminous briefs and exhibits, ne=
ither plaintiffs nor defendants seriously attempt to analyze officers' and =
directors' fiduciary duties or the business judgment rule under Texas law. =
This is particularly so in view of the authorities cited in their discussio=
ns of the business judgment rule: Smith and Gearhart argue back and forth o=
ver the applicability of the plethora of out-of-state cases they cite, yet =
they ignore the fact that we are obligated to decide these aspects of this =
case under Texas law.=E2=80=9D


  [5] Indap notes: =E2=80=9CThe fee award in this case will be more challen=
ging, however, given that Musk is simply returning shares he had been grant=
ed and that no cash is changing hands between the sides. Bernstein and two =
other law firms working with it could end up taking fees in Tesla shares, s=
ome lawyers speculated.=E2=80=9D=20

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=

5px;">Texas</h2>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I can tell you, if you want, the rules that ap=
ply to Elon Musk=E2=80=99s pay as chief executive officer of Tesla Inc. und=
er Delaware corporate law. They are long and boring, a lot of exceptions to=
 exceptions, but to quickly summarize:=C2=A0The board can pay Musk whatever=
 it wants,=C2=A0<em>unless=C2=A0</em>he is a =E2=80=9Ccontrolling sharehold=
er,=E2=80=9D in which case the pay has to be =E2=80=9Centirely fair,=E2=80=
=9D <em>though</em> if the shareholders vote to approve the pay then that p=
robably makes it =E2=80=9Centirely fair,=E2=80=9D <em>unless</em> the share=
holder vote is not fully informed, in which case a judge gets to decide if =
it was =E2=80=9Centirely fair,=E2=80=9D which requires a =E2=80=9Cfair proc=
ess=E2=80=9D in which the board negotiates with Musk at arm=E2=80=99s lengt=
h, and also a =E2=80=9Cfair price=E2=80=9D in which, you know, the judge de=
cides if Musk is worth the money.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This week a judge=C2=A0decided that Musk is=C2=
=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>worth the $55.8 billion that Tesla=E2=80=99s board agr=
eed to pay him in 2018, and wrote a <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergb=
usiness.com/click/34203294.257394/aHR0cHM6Ly9hc3NldHMuYndieC5pby9kb2N1bWVud=
HMvdXNlcnMvaXFqV0hCRmRmeElVL3JwYU9FZWZRc2UwMC92MA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB=
8da97d1b" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" =
style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wi=
dth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgro=
und-color: #ccc;">200-page opinion</a> explaining and applying those rules.=
 You can read the opinion, or <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusines=
s.com/click/34203294.257394/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2F=
ydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDEtMzEvZWxvbi1tdXNrLWlzLW92ZXJwYWlkP2NtcGlkPUJCRDAyMDEyNF=
9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90Z=
XJtPTI0MDIwMSZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB8bc9=
a4a5" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" styl=
e=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width:=
 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-=
color: #ccc;">my post about it yesterday</a>, for a fuller explanation of t=
hese rules and how the judge applied them. But I would say that my two main=
 points yesterday were:</p>
<ul style=3D"list-style-type: disc; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">There is some advantage to having a se=
t of outside rules of good behavior and fair treatment of shareholders, uph=
eld by impartial judges, rather than just letting corporate executives and =
boards and controlling shareholders do whatever they want, but</li>=09<li s=
tyle=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Nonetheless, it is=C2=A0<em>weird=C2=A0</em>th=
at Tesla=E2=80=99s management and board of directors and (a large majority =
of)=C2=A0shareholders all agreed that Musk should get paid $55.8 billion fo=
r creating $600 billion of shareholder value, and he did do that, and he go=
t paid that, and a judge overruled that decision and ordered him to give ba=
ck the money. I can see why Musk =E2=80=94=C2=A0and Tesla=E2=80=99s board, =
and its shareholders =E2=80=94=C2=A0would find that objectionable! They=E2=
=80=99re trying to run a company here.</li>
</ul>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The judge who ruled against Musk=C2=A0is the h=
ead of the <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/3420329=
4.257394/aHR0cHM6Ly9jb3VydHMuZGVsYXdhcmUuZ292L2NoYW5jZXJ5L2p1ZGdlcy5hc3B4/6=
07f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBb8e3aa77" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"items=
cope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !imp=
ortant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bott=
om-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Delaware Court of Chancery</a>, =
the specialized court in Delaware that hears corporate law disputes, and sh=
e heard the dispute about Musk=E2=80=99s pay because Tesla, like many US pu=
blic companies, is incorporated in Delaware. (Even though its factories and=
 offices are mostly located elsewhere.) Lots of public companies are incorp=
orated in Delaware, for a combination of reasons:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Delaware <em>has</em> a specialized c=
ourt that hears corporate law disputes, the Court of Chancery.=C2=A0The jud=
ges on that court (the chancellor and vice-chancellors) are=C2=A0experts, t=
hey hear a lot of corporate law disputes, they understand the issues, and t=
hey mostly make sensible decisions. They also understand that these cases a=
re time-sensitive, so they move fast. (Though in Musk=E2=80=99s case the de=
cision did take rather a long time.) They also don=E2=80=99t have <em>jurie=
s</em>.=C2=A0So if there=E2=80=99s some dispute about what a Delaware publi=
c company can do, the company knows it can go to court and get a quick answ=
er from a smart, knowledgeable judge.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5p=
x;">That court has been around=C2=A0for a long time, so there are a lot of =
precedents, so Delaware law is=C2=A0<em>predictable</em>. I=C2=A0<em>can=C2=
=A0</em>tell you the rules that apply to Elon Musk=E2=80=99s pay, and for e=
ach of the debatable terms =E2=80=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9Ccontrolling shareholder,=
=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9Centirely fair,=E2=80=9D etc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0there are ca=
ses that explain how to interpret them. Predictability=C2=A0is very importa=
nt to public companies. They don=E2=80=99t=C2=A0<em>want=C2=A0</em>to go to=
 court to get answers about what they can do: They want to=C2=A0<em>know=C2=
=A0</em>what they can do, in advance, without getting sued. If you=E2=80=99=
re a Delaware company and you have some gnarly issue, you can call pretty m=
uch any big-time corporate lawyer and say =E2=80=9Cam I allowed to do this =
gnarly thing,=E2=80=9D and she will go consult Delaware precedents and come=
 back to you with a pretty good answer.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"=
#footnote-1" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bor=
der-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: n=
one !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-b=
lock;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a>=C2=A0Note that in Delaware, as elsewhere, this is largely a matter of =
judge-made law, of precedential rulings interpreting=C2=A0the rules; it=E2=
=80=99s not like the Delaware legislature (or any other state=E2=80=99s leg=
islature) sat down and wrote detailed rules about how much=C2=A0a company c=
an pay its CEO. But compared to other states, Delaware=E2=80=99s judge-made=
 corporate law is more detailed and predictable.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin=
-bottom: 5px;">In some very general way, Delaware law is pretty business-fr=
iendly, and specifically pretty friendly to corporate management. If you as=
k your fancy lawyer =E2=80=9Cam I allowed to do this gnarly thing,=E2=80=9D=
 there=E2=80=99s a decent chance that the answer in Delaware is =E2=80=9Cye=
s.=E2=80=9D The answer in most other states is =E2=80=9CI don=E2=80=99t kno=
w=E2=80=9D; it=E2=80=99s not like the other states have rules against, say,=
 paying your CEO a lot or whatever. But that=C2=A0answer always has a tinge=
 of =E2=80=9CI don=E2=80=99t know, but it=E2=80=99s possible that some judg=
e or jury will find this thing distasteful and rule against you.=E2=80=9D W=
hereas in Delaware, the judges have been there before and are not easily af=
fronted, and there are no juries. So you can have a much more businesslike =
discussion, of the form =E2=80=9Clook I know that this pile of money we are=
 giving our CEO seems obscene, but we have good business reasons for it,=E2=
=80=9D and the judge will be sympathetic to that form of argument and often=
 agree with you. Often! Not in Musk=E2=80=99s case though.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That third point is, I want to say, probably t=
he least important factor on the list. If you are deciding where to incorpo=
rate your company, knowing that the rules will be predictable and sensible =
and enforced is really really important: There will be a lot of rules, a lo=
t of potential disputes, a lot of novel situations that you will encounter =
in running a public company, and you want to know=C2=A0what you are allowed=
 to do in those situations. Knowing that in some situation the rules will b=
e a bit more favorable =E2=80=94=C2=A0knowing that the rule is =E2=80=9Cwe =
can pay our CEO whatever we want=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0is probably less i=
mportant than knowing that=C2=A0<em>in general=C2=A0</em>the rules are pred=
ictable and reasonable, that you can run your company=E2=80=99s affairs rat=
ionally rather than by guesswork.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On the other hand, if you are another US state=
 (or a foreign jurisdiction), and you want to induce companies to incorpora=
te in your state rather than in Delaware, the=C2=A0<em>main=C2=A0</em>thing=
 that you can offer is =E2=80=9Cwe will be even more management-friendly th=
an Delaware.=E2=80=9D<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; ba=
ckground-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a>=C2=A0You can=E2=80=99t offer a deep body of precedent produced by expe=
rt judges, or not yet anyway. But you can say =E2=80=9Chey, our rules aren=
=E2=80=99t really written yet, and you can=E2=80=99t entirely predict what =
they will be, but you can reasonably guess that they will give corporate ma=
nagers more freedom and less shareholder oversight than Delaware=E2=80=99s =
rules do.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Is that an appealing pitch? Maybe? Not to ever=
yone. But you know who really values the freedom to do what he wants, and r=
eally does not care about predictability? Bloomberg=E2=80=99s <a href=3D"ht=
tps://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34203294.257394/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3c=
uYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDEtMzEvbXVzay1pcy1nZXR0aW5nLW=
EtcmVzb3VuZGluZy15ZXMtZm9yLXRleGFzLWFmdGVyLXBheS1yZWplY3Rpb24_Y21waWQ9QkJEM=
DIwMTI0X01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXIm=
dXRtX3Rlcm09MjQwMjAxJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a2=
3cB85eaa062" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blan=
k" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom=
-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; back=
ground-color: #ccc;">Madlin Mekelburg reports</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Elon Musk ... plans to summon Tesla shareholde=
rs for a vote on shifting the company=E2=80=99s incorporation to Texas from=
 Delaware, after a judge in the tiny state voided his $55 billion pay packa=
ge. ...</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If Tesla follows through, such a move would am=
ount to another win for a state that has used its ties with him to burnish =
its pro-business credentials. Texas has been luring CEOs and their companie=
s for years by touting its low taxes and light regulatory touch. Being home=
 to Tesla=E2=80=99s legal incorporation would dovetail with a more recent s=
tate initiative: developing its own business-court system in a challenge to=
 Delaware. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Ditching Delaware and moving Tesla=E2=80=99s l=
egal incorporation to Texas would certainly carry risks. The First State ha=
s long been the destination of choice for companies seeking to incorporate,=
 due to a well-developed set of corporate-governance laws and 125 years of =
case decisions out of state courts that provide robust protections for boar=
d directors and executives.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Chancery court judges in the state are recogni=
zed as business-law experts who can hear cases on a fast-track basis. Most =
high-profile merger-and-acquisition disputes are litigated in Delaware in n=
on-jury cases. Even foreign companies come to the state to have corporate d=
isputes decided.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The same hasn=E2=80=99t been true in Texas, wh=
ere business law disputes are routinely pushed to make way for emergency cr=
iminal cases or family law matters. As a result, business cases sometimes t=
ake years to resolve. Moreover, the outcomes can be unpredictable, and the =
state courts have been known to grant huge awards for plaintiffs suing comp=
anies.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In an effort to streamline such proceedings, s=
tate leaders moved last year to establish dedicated business courts in majo=
r cities. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0The courts won=E2=80=99t open until September, and=
 much about how they will operate has yet to be established.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Nothing here is ever legal advice, and I have =
never been licensed to practice law in Texas (or Delaware). But I could <em=
>not</em>=C2=A0tell you the rules that would apply to Elon Musk=E2=80=99s p=
ay under Texas law, and I spent like 15=C2=A0minutes Googling them.<a class=
=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-3" style=3D"color: #000000; text-deco=
ration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00=
0000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !=
important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a>=C2=A0Here is a <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/clic=
k/34203294.257394/aHR0cHM6Ly9jYXNldGV4dC5jb20vY2FzZS9nZWFyaGFydC1pbmR1c3Rya=
WVzLWluYy12LXNtaXRoLWludGVybg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB68ab8ac1" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
1984 federal case</a> under Texas law stating that =E2=80=9Ctransactions in=
volving an interested director are subject to strict judicial scrutiny but =
are not voidable unless they are shown to be unfair to the corporation,=E2=
=80=9D which sounds a=C2=A0<em>little=C2=A0</em>like the =E2=80=9Centire fa=
irness=E2=80=9D standard that the Delaware judge applied to Musk=E2=80=99s =
pay, but I would not want to speculate on the differences between=C2=A0thos=
e standards.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">You can find articles like =E2=80=9C<a href=3D=
"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34203294.257394/aHR0cHM6Ly93=
d3cuam9uZXNkYXkuY29tL2VuL2luc2lnaHRzLzIwMTAvMDQvdGhlLWltcG9ydGFuY2Utb2YtaW5=
kZXBlbmRlbnQtYW5kLWRpc2ludGVyZXN0ZWQtZGlyZWN0b3JzLWZvci1jb3Jwb3JhdGUtbGl0aW=
dhdGlvbi1pbi10ZXhhcw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB59c555f3" itemprop=3D"StoryLi=
nk" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text=
-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color=
: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">The Importa=
nce of Independent and Disinterested Directors for Corporate Litigation in =
Texas</a>,=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9C<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusin=
ess.com/click/34203294.257394/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuancuY29tL3dwLWNvbnRlbnQvdXBsb=
2Fkcy8yMDE2LzA4LzEyMzAucGRm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB83511aa3" itemprop=3D"=
StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #00000=
0; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-botto=
m-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Fidu=
ciary Duties of Corporate Directors and Officers in Texas</a>,=E2=80=9D but=
 on questions like =E2=80=9Cwhat are the rules for what a Texas company can=
 pay its CEO who is also a 20% shareholder,=E2=80=9D those articles mostly =
cite Delaware precedents.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-4" s=
tyle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wid=
th: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important=
; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a> Because there are Delaware precedents! More than 300 of the companies =
in the S&P 500 index are incorporated in Delaware; four are incorporate=
d in Texas. If you have a question about what=E2=80=99s allowed under Delaw=
are law, there=E2=80=99s a pretty good chance that a Delaware public compan=
y has done it and gotten an answer; in Texas the chances are slim.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Still, I mean, the bet here for Elon Musk is r=
easonable: If he moves Tesla to Texas, and then demands that Tesla=E2=80=99=
s board pay him $100 billion to keep a reasonable fraction of his time and =
attention on Tesla, and Tesla=E2=80=99s extremely accommodating board says =
=E2=80=9Csure whatever you want,=E2=80=9D and a majority of shareholders ap=
prove the pay package, and one disgruntled shareholder sues, and the case g=
oes to Texas business court, and the complaining shareholder comes into cou=
rt citing conflicts of interest and the board=E2=80=99s lack of independenc=
e and the Delaware cases on =E2=80=9Centire fairness,=E2=80=9D and Elon Mus=
k comes into court saying =E2=80=9Cwell that may all be true but what you a=
re missing is that I am Elon Musk,=E2=80=9D and Texas Governor Greg Abbott =
is in the first row of spectators with a big sign saying =E2=80=9CTX <3 =
U ELON,=E2=80=9D is the Texas business court, in its first real high-profil=
e case, going to say =E2=80=9Cactually it=E2=80=99s illegal to pay Elon Mus=
k that much=E2=80=9D? It absolutely is not.=C2=A0<em>That=C2=A0</em>much is=
 pretty predictable.</p>

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A couple of other points on this week=E2=80=99=
s Delaware Chancery Court decision in=C2=A0<em>Tornetta v. Musk</em>, inval=
idating Elon Musk=E2=80=99s big pay package.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">First: =E2=80=9CThe lawyers who represented vi=
ctorious Tesla shareholders may be in line for a record-breaking payout wor=
th hundreds of millions, or even billions, of dollars,=E2=80=9D <a href=3D"=
https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34203294.257394/aHR0cHM6Ly93d=
3cuZnQuY29tL2NvbnRlbnQvOWMwMjdkYTUtNjEyZC00NmIyLTg3MGItNjg0ZTY0MGVkN2Jl/607=
f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBfabace68" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsco=
pe" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impor=
tant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom=
-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> reports Sujeet Indap</a> at the Fi=
nancial Times. It is=C2=A0<em>logically possible=C2=A0</em>for a Delaware j=
udge to conclude that (1) Elon Musk=E2=80=99s successful=C2=A0efforts, as C=
EO of Tesla, to take Tesla=E2=80=99s market value from $59 billion to $650 =
billion, were not worth $55.8 billion to Tesla shareholders, but (2) some l=
awyers=E2=80=99 successful efforts to make Musk give back the $55.8 billion=
 <em>are</em> worth billions of dollars to Tesla shareholders. And she does=
 in fact get to decide. But I think that if you put it to a vote of Tesla s=
hareholders =E2=80=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cwould you rather give Musk $55.8 billio=
n of Tesla stock for running Tesla, or these lawyers $1 billion of Tesla st=
ock<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-5" style=3D"color: #000000=
; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom=
-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: tr=
ansparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[5]</span>
</a>=C2=A0for suing Tesla?=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0you=E2=80=99d get a pret=
ty large majority for Musk? I=E2=80=99m not Musk=E2=80=99s biggest cheerlea=
der or anything, but I feel like he has probably=C2=A0done a lot more for T=
esla than these lawyers have.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Second: Can the board just give Musk=C2=A0<em>=
another=C2=A0</em>big pay package? Part of the problem that the judge found=
 with his 2018 package is that, while shareholders voted to approve it, and=
 while Tesla accurately and completely disclosed its=C2=A0<em>terms=C2=A0</=
em>to the shareholders, the disclosure omitted some bad details about the c=
onflicted=C2=A0<em>process=C2=A0</em>by which the board approved it. So the=
 shareholder approval didn=E2=80=99t exactly work. Could Tesla just try aga=
in? Include all the bad disclosure? Attach a copy of the opinion, to be lik=
e =E2=80=9Cif you want to read how bad our process was, here you go=E2=80=
=9D? Would that solve the problem? At the Wall Street Journal, <a href=3D"h=
ttps://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34203294.257394/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3=
cud3NqLmNvbS9maW5hbmNlL3N0b2Nrcy9vbmx5LXRlc2xhLXNoYXJlaG9sZGVycy1jYW4tY2xlY=
W4tdXAtbXVza3MtY29tcGVuc2F0aW9uLW1lc3MtYmU4NzZlMmM_bW9kPWl0cF93c2o/607f07ce=
ef4b8524a319a23cBe4300492" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" t=
arget=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important;=
 border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-styl=
e: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Stephen Wilmot writes</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Tesla could appeal the ruling to the Supreme C=
ourt of Delaware. Alternatively, it could feasibly take the deal back to mi=
nority shareholders with a more explicit set of disclosures regarding Musk=
=E2=80=99s role in influencing it. Hence the question: Would Tesla win appr=
oval a second time?</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The company has an unusually high share of ind=
ividual shareholders, many of whom are also die-hard Musk fans. Insiders an=
d institutions own just 57% of Tesla shares, according to FactSet, compared=
 with more than 70% for the likes of Microsoft, Nvidia and Alphabet. Some i=
nstitutional investors have said Musk=E2=80=99s extraordinary rewards were =
due payment for unlikely achievements. But others have been critical, led b=
y proxy advisers Glass Lewis and ISS.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I don=E2=80=99t know! I assume=C2=A0that Tesla=
=E2=80=99s shareholders would vote to give him the money back. It=E2=80=99s=
 a weird dynamic, though. In 2018, when shareholders approved this package,=
 they were promising Musk options worth as much as $55.8 billion if he took=
 Tesla=E2=80=99s market capitalization from $59 billion to $650 billion. Th=
ey gave him a big incentive to encourage him to do something difficult and =
valuable. But now he=E2=80=99s already done it! They don=E2=80=99t need to =
incentivize it anymore. I suppose they could vote on a new package to give =
him, like, $500 billion of stock if he takes the market cap to $6 trillion,=
 but that is not quite what he wants. Also even before this ruling he was=
=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34203294.257=
394/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDEtMTY=
vbW9yZ2FuLXN0YW5sZXktbGVha2VkLXNvbWUtYmxvY2stdHJhZGVzP2NtcGlkPUJCRDAyMDEyNF=
9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90Z=
XJtPTI0MDIwMSZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB861c=
a4da" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" styl=
e=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width:=
 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-=
color: #ccc;">asking for more stock</a> just to not take his best ideas els=
ewhere.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also, what if the shareholders=C2=A0<em>did=C2=
=A0</em>approve the pay deal again? Presumably Mr. Tornetta, the disgruntle=
d shareholder who sued in this case, would go back to court. The court woul=
d get to review the pay package again, and since Musk is still the controll=
ing shareholder, the review would still be under the =E2=80=9Centire fairne=
ss" standard. The fully informed shareholder vote would=C2=A0<em>help</em>;=
 as <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34203294.25739=
4/aHR0cHM6Ly9hc3NldHMuYndieC5pby9kb2N1bWVudHMvdXNlcnMvaXFqV0hCRmRmeElVL3JwY=
U9FZWZRc2UwMC92MA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cC8da97d1b" itemprop=3D"StoryLink"=
 itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-de=
coration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #=
000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> the judge wro=
te</a>: =E2=80=9CDelaware law allows defendants to shift the burden of proo=
f under the entire fairness standard where the transaction was approved by =
a fully informed vote of the majority of the minority stockholders.=E2=80=
=9D But she would still review the pay package for =E2=80=9Centire fairness=
=E2=80=9D; it=E2=80=99s just that now the plaintiffs would bear the burden =
of proving that it=E2=80=99s=C2=A0<em>unfair</em>, instead of the board bea=
ring the burden of proving that it=E2=80=99s fair. But she already ruled th=
at it=E2=80=99s unfair! It should be easy for the plaintiffs to prove that;=
 they can just quote her opinion.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It is possible that the rule of this case is t=
hat Tesla is not=C2=A0<em>allowed=C2=A0</em>to pay Musk $55.8 billion, no m=
atter what its shareholders think, no matter how many of them vote to appro=
ve it in a fully informed vote. It=E2=80=99s enough to make you want to mov=
e to Texas.</p>

 price it got in a <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click=

too much wind power risks overloading the system, and the grid operator mus=
t respond by paying some firms not to generate. This =E2=80=9Ccurtailment=
=E2=80=9D costs consumers hundreds of millions of pounds each year.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Adding to that expense, some wind farm operato=
rs exaggerate how much energy they say they intend to produce, which boosts=
 the payments they receive for turning off, according to nine people =E2=80=
=94 traders, academics and market experts =E2=80=94 most of whom agreed to =
discuss this controversial behavior only on condition of anonymity.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In effect, they said, the grid has paid some w=
ind farms not to generate power that they wouldn=E2=80=99t have produced an=
yway.</p>
</blockquote>

=C2=A0Gavin Finch, Todd Gillespie, Eric Fan, Jason Grotto and Sam Dodge. Th=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If a door flies off an airplane in midair, is =

toryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000=
; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom=
-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Say i=
t with me now</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Rhode Island=E2=80=99s general treasurer filed=
 a class action lawsuit against The Boeing Company on Tuesday, alleging it =
betrayed the trust of the state=E2=80=99s pensioners.=C2=A0=E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Filed in a U.S. District Court in Virginia, th=
e civil case comes less than a month after a door plug blew out on a Boeing=
 737 Max 9 midflight and a string of other mishaps raised concerns about th=
e company=E2=80=99s planes. The suit alleges Boeing issued false and mislea=
ding statements to the market about its safety lapses.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The suit alleges Boeing assured investors it w=
as focused on safety after Max plane crashes in 2018 and 2019 killed 346 pe=
ople.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">See the problem is that Boeing=C2=A0<em>said <=
/em>it was trying to build safe planes, but=C2=A0<em>in fact=C2=A0</em>was =
building planes whose doors fly off. From <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloo=
mbergbusiness.com/click/34203294.257394/aHR0cHM6Ly9hc3NldHMuYndieC5pby9kb2N=
1bWVudHMvdXNlcnMvaXFqV0hCRmRmeElVL3JHTk1uZVNHeDR0cy92MA/607f07ceef4b8524a31=
9a23cBc6e8f99c" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_b=
lank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bot=
tom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; b=
ackground-color: #ccc;"> the complaint</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Class Period starts on October 23, 2019, w=
hen Defendants boasted that Boeing was =E2=80=9Cmaking steady progress,=E2=
=80=9D on its =E2=80=9Ctop priority . . . the safe return to service of the=
 737 MAX.=E2=80=9D Throughout the Class Period, Defendants made similar sta=
tements, consistently assuring investors that Boeing was =E2=80=9Claser-foc=
used on . . . safety, quality and transparency.=E2=80=9D Defendants also cl=
aimed that Boeing did not make =E2=80=9Ctrade-offs,=E2=80=9D between safety=
 and profit, and that safety has =E2=80=9Calways been the priority and that=
 will continue to be=E2=80=9D at Boeing.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Unbeknownst to investors, statements such as t=
hose above were false and misleading because Boeing failed to disclose that=
 it had been prioritizing its profits over safety, which led to poor qualit=
y control standards in the production of its commercial aircrafts such as t=
he 737 MAX, resulting in a heightened risk of manufacturing flaws which cou=
ld render the Company=E2=80=99s new airplanes unsafe. This very risk had ma=
terialized during the Class Period.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbus=
iness.com/click/34203294.257394/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cudm94LmNvbS8yMDI0LzEvOC8yNDA=

m-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">got =
$1,500 of compensation</a> for the trauma and inconvenience of being on a p=
lane whose door flew off in midair. I suppose some might sue for more. If t=

ecause he is a controlling shareholder, but that is not generally true, and=
 the first page of her opinion says =E2=80=9CA board of director=E2=80=99s =
decision on how much to pay a company=E2=80=99s chief executive officer is =
the quintessential business determination subject to great judicial deferen=
ce.=E2=80=9D So I will not trust ChatGPT on this one.</p>
</div>
<div id=3D"footnote-4" class=3D"footnote-content" style=3D"font-style: ital=
ic;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">[4] Incidentally, in that <a href=3D"https://l=

order-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style:=
 solid; background-color: #ccc;">1984 federal case</a>, the court complaine=
d in a footnote: =E2=80=9CWe are both surprised and inconvenienced by the c=
ircumstance that, despite their multitudinous and voluminous briefs and exh=
ibits, neither plaintiffs nor defendants seriously attempt to analyze offic=
ers' and directors' fiduciary duties or the business judgment rule under Te=
xas law. This is particularly so in view of the authorities cited in their =
discussions of the business judgment rule: Smith and Gearhart argue back an=
d forth over the applicability of the plethora of out-of-state cases they c=
ite, yet they ignore the fact that we are obligated to decide these aspects=
 of this case under Texas law.=E2=80=9D</p>
